front: Film: ends with a celebrated freeze-frame of Antoine Doinel's face as he runs toward the sea after escaping juvenile detention
back: The 400 Blows / Les Quatre Cents Coups (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Antoine steals a typewriter and is sent to an observation center for juvenile delinquents
back: The 400 Blows / Les Quatre Cents Coups (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Antoine's shrine to Balzac catches fire; he is accused of plagiarizing an essay by a character called 'Sourpuss'
back: The 400 Blows / Les Quatre Cents Coups (François Truffaut)

front: Film: dedicated to André Bazin's memory; considered one of the founding works of the French New Wave
back: The 400 Blows / Les Quatre Cents Coups (François Truffaut)

front: Film: contains an improvised interrogation scene where Antoine is questioned about trying to return a stolen typewriter
back: The 400 Blows / Les Quatre Cents Coups (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Antoine Doinel (Jean-Pierre Léaud) and Catherine: Jules is Austrian, Jim is French; Catherine jumps into a river after feeling excluded from a discussion of Strindberg
back: Jules and Jim / Jules et Jim (François Truffaut)

front: Film: a character sings 'La Marseillaise' to disprove having an accent, which fades into a WWI montage
back: Jules and Jim / Jules et Jim (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Truffaut cast himself as a WWI veteran obsessing over a shrine to deceased friends in a burned-out chapel; based on Henry James's 'The Altar of the Dead'
back: The Green Room / La Chambre Verte (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Julien Davenne obsesses over a shrine to his dead wife; considered an homage to André Bazin
back: The Green Room / La Chambre Verte (François Truffaut)

front: Film: meta-film about making a fictional melodrama ('Meet Pamela'); features a dream sequence of a boy stealing Citizen Kane promotional stills
back: Day for Night / La Nuit américaine (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Truffaut's only English-language feature; firemen set books ablaze; starred Oskar Werner; omits Bradbury's mechanical hound
back: Fahrenheit 451 (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Charles Aznavour plays Eddie Saroyan/Charlie, a pianist concealing his identity from pursuing gangsters; karaoke captions display a Boby Lapointe song about 'Raspberry'
back: Shoot the Piano Player / Tirez sur le pianiste (François Truffaut)
work: Shoot the Piano Player (Tirez sur le pianiste, 1960)

front: Film: Antoine Doinel uses a shoe store job as cover for private detective work; opens with a 'closed' sign at the Cinémathèque française
back: Stolen Kisses (François Truffaut)

front: Film: Antoine Doinel marries Christine Darbon (a violin instructor) after meeting her in Stolen Kisses; has affair with Japanese woman Kyoko
back: Bed and Board (François Truffaut)

front: Director: pioneer of auteur theory; 'A Certain Tendency of the French Cinema' essay attacked quality French cinema and destroyed the careers of the screenwriting duo 'Aurenchebost'
back: François Truffaut

front: Director: first to use the word 'auteur' in a film-theoretic sense; wrote for Cahiers du Cinéma
back: François Truffaut

front: Director: called 'The Gravedigger of French Cinema' for his brutal reviews
back: François Truffaut

front: Director: published a landmark book-length interview with Alfred Hitchcock (1966), compiled with help from former spy Helen Grace Scott
back: François Truffaut

front: Director: played a scientist in Close Encounters of the Third Kind who conveys notes D, E, C, C, G to an alien via hand signs
back: François Truffaut

front: Director: with Godard, called for the cancellation of the 1968 Cannes Film Festival during the May '68 protests
back: François Truffaut

front: Director: praised Nicholas Ray's Johnny Guitar as 'The Beauty and the Beast of Westerns'
back: François Truffaut
